Case: A 6-year-old boy is brought to the neurological clinic by his mother with complaints of 'staring off' in the home as well as during the classes in the school. These episodes last for few seconds and are associated with lip smacking. He has been struggling in the school. He was prescribed ethosuximide. His school performance has improved significantly since then.

Q1a. What is the difference between a seizure and epilepsy?
correctAnswer: A seizure is a sudden, uncontrolled electrical disturbance in the brain that can cause changes in behavior, movements or feelings, and in levels of consciousness. Epilepsy is defined as having two or more seizures or a tendency to have recurrent seizures.
sourceAnswer: A seizure is a sudden, uncontrolled electrical disturbance in the brain. It can cause changes in the behavior, movements or feelings, and in levels of consciousness. If there are two or more seizures or a tendency to have recurrent seizures, then it is an epilepsy.

question:
Case: A 6-year-old boy is brought to the neurological clinic by his mother with complaints of 'staring off' in the home as well as during the classes in the school. These episodes last for few seconds and are associated with lip smacking.

Q1b. Describe different types of generalized seizures and their clinical presentations.
correctAnswer: Absence seizures (petit mal): blank out or stare into space for a few seconds, most common in children. Myoclonic seizures: brief shock-like jerks of a muscle or group of muscles; person is usually awake and able to think clearly. Clonic seizures: jerking of the body or parts of the body; movements cannot be stopped by restraining. Tonic seizures: body, arms, or legs suddenly stiff or tense. Tonic-clonic seizures (grand mal): involve both tonic (stiffening) and clonic (twitching or jerking) phases of muscle activity. Atonic seizures: sudden lack of muscle tone can cause the person to fall; person usually remains conscious.
sourceAnswer: Absence seizures (petit mal seizures): blank out or stare into space for a few seconds, most common in children. Myoclonic seizures: brief shock-like jerks of a muscle or group of muscles; during a seizure the person is usually awake and able to think clearly. Clonic seizures: jerking of the body or parts of the body are the main symptom; clonic seizure movements cannot be stopped by restraining. Tonic seizures: the body, arms, or legs may be suddenly stiff or tense. Tonic-clonic seizures (grand mal): involve both tonic (stiffening) and clonic (twitching or jerking) phases. Atonic seizures: sudden lack of muscle strength or tone can cause the person to fall to the ground; person usually remains conscious.

question:
Case: A 6-year-old boy is brought to the neurological clinic with 'staring off' episodes and lip smacking.

Q1c. Describe the clinical presentation of partial seizures.
correctAnswer: Simple partial: consciousness is intact; may involve motor, sensory, autonomic, and psychic symptoms. Complex partial: impaired consciousness and automatism.
sourceAnswer: Simple partial: Conscious intact. May involve motor, sensory, autonomic, and psychic symptoms. Complex partial: Impaired conscious and automatism.

question:
Case: A 6-year-old boy presents with episodes of 'staring off' for few seconds associated with lip smacking, prescribed ethosuximide with improvement.

Q1d. Why does this patient have 'staring off' episodes?
correctAnswer: He has absence seizures, which characteristically present with brief staring spells lasting a few seconds, often associated with automatisms such as lip smacking.

question:
Case: A 6-year-old boy with absence seizures was prescribed ethosuximide with significant improvement.

Q1e. Describe the mode of action of ethosuximide and how it is useful in this case.
correctAnswer: Ethosuximide blocks T-type Ca2+ channels in the thalamus, thus stopping the hypersynchronous thalamocortical activity responsible for absence seizures.
sourceAnswer: It blocks the T-type Ca2+-channels in the thalamus, thus stopping the hypersynchronous activity of the thalamus which is causing this general seizure (absence).

question:
Case: A 6-year-old boy with absence seizures.

Q1f. Discuss the mode of actions, indications, and side effects of two antiepileptic drugs.
correctAnswer:
1. Phenytoin: Indicated for generalized seizures, partial seizures, and status epilepticus. MOA: prolongs the inactivation phase of Na+ channels. Side effects: gingival hyperplasia, peripheral neuropathy.
2. Valproic acid: Indicated for absence seizures, myoclonic seizures, and as second-line for partial seizures. MOA: blocks T-type Ca2+ channels in the thalamus. Side effects: hepatotoxicity (via metabolic product), spina bifida (teratogenic in pregnant women).
sourceAnswer: Phenytoin: indicated for generalized, partial and status epilepticus. MCA: prolongs the inactivation phase of Na channels. Side effects: gingival hyperplasia, peripheral neuropathy. Valproic acid: indicated for absence, myoclonic and second line for partial seizures. MCA: blocks the T-type Ca2+-channels in the thalamus. Side effects: hepatotoxicity by the metabolic product, spina bifida if taken by pregnant women.

question:
Case: Mr. Ahmed, a 44-year-old businessman, presented with 4-hour history of vertigo, nausea, and vomiting. He has a 40-pack-year smoking history and BP 160/90 mmHg. Three hours later he developed slurred speech, numbness and paresthesia on the right side of his face and left side of trunk and extremities, and dysphagia to liquids.

Q2a. Which area of the brain do you suspect to be involved in this case?
correctAnswer: Lateral medulla (Wallenberg syndrome / lateral medullary syndrome).

question:
Case: Mr. Ahmed, 44-year-old with lateral medullary stroke, 40-pack-year smoking history, BP 160/90 mmHg.

Q2b. Describe two modifiable and two non-modifiable risk factors for stroke, and identify which are present in this case.
correctAnswer: Modifiable (present in this case): Smoking (40-pack-year history), Hypertension (BP 160/90 mmHg). Non-modifiable (present in this case): Sex (male), Age (44 years old).
sourceAnswer: Modifiable: Smoking, Hypertension. Non-modifiable: Sex, Age.

question:
Case: Mr. Ahmed, 44-year-old with suspected stroke and findings including right-sided ptosis/miosis, decreased facial sensation on right, absent gag reflex, left-sided pain/temperature loss below neck, right-sided dysmetria, and truncal ataxia.

Q2c. What would be the first radiological investigation to be done in this case?
correctAnswer: Non-contrast CT scan of the head, to rule out hemorrhagic stroke before any other management.

question:
Case: Mr. Ahmed, 44-year-old with suspected lateral medullary stroke.

Q2d. Which diagnostic radiological test will confirm the diagnosis and what findings do you suspect on this test?
correctAnswer: MRI with DWI (Diffusion-Weighted Imaging). Expected findings: hyperintense (bright) signal in the affected area on DWI indicating acute ischemia. ADC map: hypointense (dark) in acute ischemia (restricted diffusion), hyperintense in chronic ischemia.
sourceAnswer: MRI (DWI) — it will show a hyperdense lesion in the area if there is ischemia. We can further assess the age of the ischemia by ADC: if hypodense (dark) then it is acute ischemia; if bright then it is chronic.
explanation: Note: On DWI, acute ischemia appears bright (hyperintense). On the ADC map, acute ischemia appears dark (hypointense) due to restricted diffusion. The source answer uses 'hyperdense' loosely to mean 'bright on DWI.'

question:
Case: Mr. Ahmed, 44-year-old with lateral medullary syndrome (right-sided ptosis/miosis, ipsilateral facial sensory loss, absent gag reflex, contralateral body pain/temperature loss, ipsilateral dysmetria, truncal ataxia).

Q2e. Which artery is involved in this case?
sourceAnswer: Right Posterior Inferior Cerebellar Artery PICA.

question:
Case: Mr. Ahmed, 44-year-old with right PICA (lateral medullary) stroke. Clinical findings:
1. Right-sided ptosis and miosis
2. Pinprick and temperature sensation decreased throughout the right face
3. Gag reflex absent
4. Deep tendon and superficial reflexes normal
5. Sensation to light touch and vibration normal in all extremities
6. Sensations to pain and temperature decreased below the neck on the left side
7. Severe right-sided dysmetria on finger-to-nose and heel-to-shin testing
8. Marked truncal ataxia with tendency to fall to the right

Q2f. Give the reason (name the structure involved) for each of the above examination findings.
correctAnswer:
1. Right-sided ptosis and miosis — Descending sympathetic fibers (Horner's syndrome)
2. Decreased pinprick/temperature on right face — Spinal trigeminal nucleus and tract
3. Absent gag reflex — Nucleus ambiguus (CN IX/X)
4. Normal deep tendon and superficial reflexes — Medial lemniscus and corticospinal tracts spared (medially located, outside PICA territory)
5. Normal light touch and vibration — Medial lemniscus spared (medially located)
6. Decreased pain and temperature left body — Lateral spinothalamic tract
7. Right-sided dysmetria — Inferior cerebellar peduncle and right dentate nucleus (right cerebellar hemisphere)
8. Truncal ataxia, falling to the right — Inferior cerebellar peduncle, vermis, and right paravermal area
sourceAnswer: 1. Sympathetic 2. Spinal trigeminal 3. Nucleus ambiguous 4. Medial lemniscus & Corticospinal (afferent & efferent) (medially located) 5. Medial lemniscus (medially located) 6. Lateral spinothalamic 7. Inferior cerebellar peduncle & right dentate nucleus (right cerebellar hemisphere) 8. Inferior cerebellar peduncle and vermis and right paravermal area
suggestedAnswer: For findings 4 and 5: In lateral medullary (PICA) syndrome, the medial lemniscus and corticospinal tracts are medially located and therefore SPARED (not involved). The source answer lists them as the structures involved, but in this context they are mentioned to explain WHY these functions are normal — they are not damaged. The explanation should read: 'these are spared because they are medially located outside the PICA territory.'
explanation: The lateral medullary syndrome (Wallenberg syndrome) is caused by occlusion of the PICA or the vertebral artery. Structures in the lateral medulla are affected; medially located structures (medial lemniscus, corticospinal tract/pyramid) are spared, which explains the absence of motor deficits and preservation of fine touch/vibration.

question:
A 14-year-old with poor performance in school has episodes of 'staring off'. After seeing the physician, he was started on a medication called ethosuximide.

A. What is the difference between epilepsy and seizure?
B. Give 2 examples of epilepsy with their clinical presentation.
C. Why is the patient experiencing episodes of 'staring off'?
D. What is the mechanism of action of ethosuximide and how was it useful in this case?
correctAnswer:
A. Seizure: abnormal hyperactivity and overactivation of neurons. Epilepsy is a condition in which patients experience multiple recurrent seizures.

B. (1) Absence seizures: Young patient experiencing very brief and temporary loss of consciousness without any post-ictal confusion. (2) Generalized tonic-clonic seizure: 2 phases — Tonic phase: patient loses consciousness and falls down, body becomes stiff and rigid; Clonic phase: classical jerking movements of all limbs.

C. 'Staring off' is characteristic of absence seizures, where the patient experiences a very brief loss of consciousness which appears from the outside as if the patient was lost in thought staring off in the distance.

D. Ethosuximide inhibits T-type calcium channels in the thalamus, preventing the rhythmic thalamocortical discharges (3 Hz spike-and-wave) responsible for absence seizures, and therefore prevents their onset.
sourceAnswer: A. Seizure: abnormal hyperactivity & overactivation of neurons; epilepsy is condition in which patients experience multiple seizures. B. Absence seizures and Generalized tonic-clonic seizure (with descriptions). C. 'Staring off' is characteristic of absence seizures — very brief loss of consciousness appearing as staring. D. Ethosuximide inhibits T-Type calcium channels in the thalamus and therefore prevents the onset of absence seizures.
explanation: Absence epilepsy (petit mal) is most common in children. EEG shows 3 Hz spike-and-wave discharges. Ethosuximide is first-line for pure absence seizures. It is not effective for other seizure types.

question:
A 43-year-old woman is brought to the ER because of a sudden and severe headache (worst headache of her life). Her BP is 160/90 mmHg and pulse is 90/min. Her pupils are symmetric and reactive to light, and she can move all four limbs without weakness. A CT of the head is shown (star-shaped hyperdensity in basal cisterns).

A. List the components of the Circle of Willis.
B. What is the cause of this presentation of the patient?
C. Discuss the different types of intracranial hemorrhages based on their clinical presentation.
correctAnswer:
A. Components of the Circle of Willis: Anterior communicating artery (Acom), Posterior communicating arteries (Pcom) bilaterally, Internal carotid arteries, Anterior cerebral arteries (ACA) bilaterally, Posterior cerebral arteries (PCA) bilaterally. (Also: Middle cerebral arteries and basilar artery are adjacent structures.)

B. Rupture of a saccular (berry) aneurysm causing subarachnoid hemorrhage.

C. (1) Epidural hemorrhage: rupture of middle meningeal artery (from temporal bone fracture); patient experiences a lucid interval then rapid deterioration; biconvex (lens-shaped) hyperdensity on CT. (2) Subdural hemorrhage: rupture of bridging veins; crescent-shaped hemorrhage on CT; seen in elderly after minor head trauma; chronic form presents weeks after injury. (3) Subarachnoid hemorrhage: star/starburst-shaped blood in basal cisterns on CT; usually due to ruptured saccular aneurysm; patient presents with 'worst headache of their life'; spontaneous onset. (4) Intraparenchymal hemorrhage: usually due to rupture of Charcot-Bouchard microaneurysms secondary to hypertension; patient presents with focal neurological deficits depending on site; most commonly in basal ganglia (especially putamen).
sourceAnswer: A. Acom, Pcom, internal carotid, ACA, PCA. B. Rupture of Saccular aneurysm. C. Epidural (rupture of middle meningeal artery, lucid interval, biconvex on CT), Subdural (rupture of bridging veins, crescent-shaped, chronic in elderly), Subarachnoid (star-shaped, ruptured saccular aneurysm, worst headache of life), Intraparenchymal (Charcot-Bouchard microaneurysm, hypertension, basal ganglia/putamen).
explanation: Subarachnoid hemorrhage classically presents with sudden-onset 'thunderclap' headache. The CT shows hyperdense blood in the subarachnoid space, filling the basal cisterns in a star pattern. Lumbar puncture shows xanthochromia if CT is negative.

question:
The following image shows different EEG brain waves labeled A (high frequency, low amplitude), B (medium frequency), C (mixed frequency), and D (low frequency, high amplitude), plus a second diagram showing a recording tracing with two marked points A and B.

A. In the first diagram, which wave shows the EEG waveform during REM sleep? Mention its name.
B. In the first diagram, which EEG waveform is normal in awake infants but indicates pathology in awake adults? Mention its name.
C. In the first diagram, which EEG waveform is seen in adults who are awake with their eyes closed? Mention its name.
D. In the second diagram, the patient was asked to close his eyes. After some time, he was asked to perform some mental math. At which point (A or B) does the mental activity start?
correctAnswer:
A. Wave A; Beta wave (high frequency 13-30 Hz, low amplitude — characteristic of active mental activity including REM sleep).

B. Wave D; Delta wave (low frequency <4 Hz, high amplitude — normal in infant brains but indicates brain pathology/encephalopathy in awake adults).

C. Wave B; Alpha wave (8-13 Hz — seen in relaxed, awake adults with eyes closed; disappears on eye opening or mental activity).

D. Point A — at this point the alpha waves diminish and are replaced by beta waves, indicating the start of mental activity (alpha-blocking response).
sourceAnswer: A. A; beta wave. B. D; delta wave. C. B; Alpha wave. D. Point A.
explanation: EEG wave hierarchy by frequency: Beta (>13 Hz, alert/REM) > Alpha (8-13 Hz, relaxed with eyes closed) > Theta (4-8 Hz, drowsy/children) > Delta (<4 Hz, deep sleep/infants). Alpha waves are blocked by eye opening or mental activity (alpha-blocking reaction).

question: The posterior inferior cerebellar artery (PICA) arises from which of the main arteries marked as A, B, C, and D in the provided cerebral angiogram image?
explanation: PICA (posterior inferior cerebellar artery) arises from the vertebral artery. Occlusion causes lateral medullary (Wallenberg) syndrome: ipsilateral facial pain/temp loss, ipsilateral Horner's, dysphagia, dysarthria, vertigo, and contralateral body pain/temp loss.

question: After gaining consciousness, a stroke patient was examined which reveals spastic muscle paralysis in his left upper and lower limb. Mark the area affected on the provided CT scan.
correctAnswer: Right internal capsule (Posterior limb or Genu). The corticospinal fibers for the left upper and lower limbs pass through the posterior limb of the right internal capsule. A lesion here (supplied by lenticulostriate branches of the right MCA) causes contralateral spastic hemiplegia.
sourceAnswer: Right internal capsule (Posterior limb or Genu)
explanation: The internal capsule posterior limb carries corticospinal (motor) and corticobulbar fibers. Because fibers decussate in the medullary pyramids, a right-sided lesion causes left-sided spastic hemiplegia. Spasticity (UMN pattern) reflects the intact spinal cord below the lesion.

question: A 30-year-old patient, who had recovered from tuberculous meningitis, came to the clinic with severe headache, nausea and vomiting. His MRI reveals hydrocephalus. Which of the structures labelled as A, B, C, and D in the provided sagittal brain diagram will be affected in this case to cause hydrocephalus?
correctAnswer: A (arachnoid granulations). Post-tuberculous meningitis causes fibrosis and blockage of arachnoid granulations, impairing CSF reabsorption into the dural venous sinuses, resulting in communicating hydrocephalus.
explanation: Arachnoid granulations (Pacchionian bodies) are the primary sites of CSF reabsorption into the superior sagittal sinus. Inflammation and scarring from TB meningitis can obstruct these granulations, causing communicating (non-obstructive) hydrocephalus with raised ICP.

question: On sensory examination of a patient, there is loss of pain and temperature sensation in his left lower limb. Which one of the labelled tracts in the provided spinal cord cross-section diagram transmits pain and temperature?
correctAnswer: I (lateral spinothalamic tract). The lateral spinothalamic tract transmits pain and temperature from the contralateral side of the body (fibers decussate in the anterior white commissure within 1-2 spinal cord levels of entry).
explanation: Primary afferents for pain and temperature (A-delta and C fibers) enter the dorsal horn, synapse in the substantia gelatinosa (Rexed laminae I, II), and then decussate to ascend in the contralateral lateral spinothalamic tract to the VPL nucleus of the thalamus.

question:
After a stroke, a patient visited a speech therapy clinic with a major impairment of language comprehension. Although speech retains natural-sounding rhythm and normal syntax, it is meaningless.

a. Which one of the labelled cortical areas is affected in this case?
b. Name the artery involved in this case.
correctAnswer: a. C (Wernicke's area — posterior superior temporal gyrus, Brodmann area 22, dominant hemisphere). b. Inferior division of the Middle cerebral artery (MCA).
sourceAnswer: a. C (Wernicke's area). b. Inferior division of Middle cerebral artery.
explanation: Wernicke's (receptive/fluent) aphasia: fluent but meaningless speech (jargon aphasia), severely impaired comprehension, impaired repetition. Caused by lesion in Wernicke's area supplied by the inferior division of the left MCA.

question: Which one of the labelled cells A, B, C, D in the provided cerebellar histology image is responsible for secondary motor function of the body?
correctAnswer: C (Purkinje neurons). Purkinje cells are the sole output neurons of the cerebellar cortex; they project inhibitory (GABAergic) signals to the deep cerebellar nuclei, modulating motor coordination.
explanation: Purkinje cells are large neurons in the cerebellar cortex with elaborate dendritic trees. They are the only neurons to send axons out of the cerebellar cortex (to deep cerebellar nuclei). They are essential for the cerebellum's role in motor coordination (secondary motor function).

question: A 60-year-old man walked into your clinic with stooped posture and rigid facial expressions. He complains of difficulty in walking for a couple of months and resting tremors. He also mentions difficulty in initiating and performing new movements. On examination, his limbs showed rigidity on passive movement. There is no loss of muscle power and deep tendon reflexes are normal. Which of the marked areas as 1, 2, 3, 4 and 5 in the provided midbrain section is affected in this case?
correctAnswer: 1 (substantia nigra pars compacta). Loss of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra causes the cardinal features of Parkinson's disease: resting tremor, rigidity, bradykinesia, and postural instability.
explanation: Parkinson's disease results from degeneration of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra pars compacta, reducing dopamine input to the striatum. This shifts the balance toward inhibition of thalamic motor output, causing the hypokinetic movement disorder.

question: A 45-year-old patient presented with a history of difficulty in swallowing. On examination, there is also loss of taste sensation in posterior 1/3 of the tongue. Which one of the labelled nerves A, B, C, D in the provided image is affected in this case?
correctAnswer: B (Glossopharyngeal nerve — CN IX). CN IX provides motor innervation to the stylopharyngeus (swallowing) and carries taste and general sensation from the posterior 1/3 of the tongue, as well as sensory from the pharynx.
explanation: CN IX (glossopharyngeal nerve) carries: (1) taste and general sensation from posterior 1/3 of tongue, (2) motor to stylopharyngeus muscle (swallowing), (3) parasympathetic to parotid gland, and (4) visceral sensation from carotid body/sinus.

question:
A 65-year-old female presented with weakness of right arm, leg, and lower half of her face. Babinski sign was present. Examination also shows ptosis of the left eyelid. When the neurologist lifted her eyelid, the pupil was dilated, and the eye was turned down and out.

a. Based on the signs and symptoms above, which of the sections marked as A, B, C, D, E, F shows the correct lesion in the attached image?
b. Name the artery involved in this case.
correctAnswer: a. D (midbrain). b. Superior cerebellar artery. This is Weber's syndrome: ipsilateral CN III palsy (ptosis, mydriasis, down-and-out eye — left side) + contralateral hemiplegia (right arm, leg, lower face) due to crus cerebri involvement.
sourceAnswer: a. D (midbrain). b. Superior cerebellar artery.
explanation: Weber's syndrome is caused by a midbrain infarction affecting the crus cerebri and CN III fascicles. Ipsilateral CN III palsy (dilated pupil, ptosis, eye deviated down and out) with contralateral upper motor neuron hemiplegia. The superior cerebellar artery and PCA branches supply the midbrain.

question: A 44-year-old lady with a 5-year history of lumbar back pain presented to the emergency department with severe sharp back pain now radiating into both her buttocks. She also complained of urinary incontinence. On examination, motor strength of her left leg was decreased to 3/5. A digital rectal examination revealed loss of sensation in the perianal area with reduced anal tone. Which of the marked areas A, B, C, and D will be affected in this case?
correctAnswer: C (Cauda Equina). Cauda equina syndrome presents with bilateral leg weakness, perianal/saddle anesthesia, urinary incontinence/retention, and reduced anal tone — caused by compression of the cauda equina nerve roots (typically by large central disc herniation at L4/L5 or L5/S1).
explanation: Cauda equina syndrome is a surgical emergency. The cauda equina consists of L2-S5 nerve roots. Compression causes lower motor neuron signs (flaccid weakness), saddle anesthesia (S3-S5 dermatomes), and sphincter dysfunction. Contrast with conus medullaris syndrome which may show mixed UMN/LMN signs.

question: Identify the structures marked as A and B in the provided midsagittal brain section image.
correctAnswer: A: Fourth ventricle. B: Aqueduct of Sylvius (cerebral aqueduct).
sourceAnswer: a. Fourth ventricle. b. Aqueduct of Sylvius.
explanation: The cerebral aqueduct (of Sylvius) connects the third ventricle to the fourth ventricle. The fourth ventricle lies between the pons/medulla anteriorly and the cerebellum posteriorly. Obstruction of the aqueduct causes non-communicating (obstructive) hydrocephalus.

question: A 45-year-old hypertensive patient presented with sudden freezing painful sensations in her left arm. In addition, she also complains of weakness and numbness down the whole of her left side. When examined locally she was weak and numb on the left side with chorea of the left arm. Which structure marked as A, B, C, D is affected in this case?
correctAnswer: D (Thalamus). Thalamic syndrome (Dejerine-Roussy syndrome): contralateral hemisensory loss, followed by spontaneous burning/freezing pain (thalamic pain), and sometimes contralateral hemiparesis and involuntary movements (choreoathetosis) due to thalamic infarction.
explanation: The thalamus (VPL/VPM nuclei) is the main sensory relay nucleus. Thalamic infarction causes Dejerine-Roussy syndrome: initial contralateral hemianesthesia followed by severe, burning central pain. Hypertension is a major risk factor. The posterior thalamus is supplied by the posterior communicating artery and thalamogeniculate branches of the PCA.

question: Identify the structures marked as 3 and 4 in the provided gross brain section image.
correctAnswer: Structure 3: Trigeminal nerve (CN V). Structure 4: Cerebellum (Dentate Nucleus). (Note: Answer contains uncertainty markers in source — see explanation.)
sourceAnswer: 1: Basilar Artery, 2: Pons, 3: Trigeminal Nerve (?), 4: Cerebellum (Dentate Nucleus?)
suggestedAnswer: Structure 3 may be the Trigeminal nerve (CN V) and structure 4 the Cerebellum/Dentate nucleus — but these identifications carry uncertainty per the source document (marked with '?').
explanation: The source document explicitly marks these answers with question marks, indicating uncertainty. The image shows a cross-section at the pons level with adjacent cerebellum. Structure 3 emerging from the lateral pons is most consistent with the trigeminal nerve (CN V, the largest cranial nerve). Structure 4 in the cerebellar parenchyma would be the dentate nucleus (largest deep cerebellar nucleus).

question: Which of the following biochemical/cytological profile in CSF analysis would you expect if the microscopic examination with India ink shows the attached image (encapsulated budding yeast organisms with large halos)?
choices: A. Opening pressure moderately increased, WBC increased 50% lymphocytes, glucose slightly decreased
B. Opening pressure elevated (450mm H2O), protein decreased, Gram stain negative
C. Gram stain positive, glucose significantly decreased, protein normal
D. Increased WBC predominantly neutrophils, glucose increased, opening pressure normal
E. Glucose normal, WBC increased, 70% lymphocytes, protein slightly elevated
explanation: India ink preparation showing encapsulated budding yeast with large halos is diagnostic of Cryptococcus neoformans meningitis. CSF profile: moderately elevated opening pressure, lymphocytic pleocytosis (predominantly lymphocytes), mildly decreased glucose, elevated protein. Cryptococcal antigen (CrAg) is highly sensitive. Seen in immunocompromised patients (HIV/AIDS, CD4 <100).

question: Which of the organisms marked as 1 (Gram stain showing Gram-positive rods), 2 (Gram stain), 3 (Gram stain), and 4 (China ink stain) is related to neonatal meningitis and is associated with the consumption of raw dairy products during pregnancy?
explanation: Listeria monocytogenes is a Gram-positive rod that causes neonatal meningitis (acquired transplacentally or during birth). It is associated with consumption of raw/unpasteurized dairy products, deli meats, and soft cheeses during pregnancy. It is one of the classic causes of neonatal meningitis along with GBS (Streptococcus agalactiae) and E. coli.

question: A medical student recorded the EEG waves in a relaxed, calm, and quiet laboratory setting while he was awake with his eyes closed. While recording he was asked to open his eyes. Which of the EEG wave tracings A, B, C, D will diminish in frequency?
explanation: Alpha waves (8-13 Hz) are predominant in relaxed, awake adults with eyes closed. Opening the eyes causes alpha-blocking (alpha desynchronization): alpha waves diminish and are replaced by lower-amplitude beta waves as the visual cortex and other areas become active.

question: What will be the most probable motor deficit if the vessels marked with a red circle in the attached picture (lenticulostriate branch of the middle cerebral artery) are occluded? Mark the illustration below which more accurately shows the motor deficit (purple area indicates motor loss).
correctAnswer: Occlusion of the lenticulostriate branch of the middle cerebral artery causes infarction of the posterior limb of the internal capsule. This produces contralateral pure motor hemiplegia affecting the face, arm, and leg equally (dense hemiplegia of the entire contralateral side).
sourceAnswer: Lenticulostriate branch of middle cerebral artery (supplies posterior limb of the internal capsule).
explanation: The lenticulostriate arteries are small perforating branches of the M1 segment of the MCA. They supply the basal ganglia and posterior limb of the internal capsule. Occlusion (classic site for hypertensive lacunar infarcts) causes a dense, contralateral pure motor hemiplegia involving face, arm, and leg.

question: Which group of neurons marked as A, B, C in the provided spinal cord histology image is involved in case of poliomyelitis?
correctAnswer: A (Anterior horn cells — lower motor neurons). Poliovirus selectively destroys anterior horn motor neurons (LMNs), causing flaccid paralysis with muscle wasting, areflexia, and fasciculations, without sensory loss.
explanation: Poliovirus (enterovirus) spreads via the fecal-oral route and selectively infects and destroys anterior horn cells (alpha motor neurons) in the spinal cord and brainstem. This causes asymmetric, flaccid LMN paralysis. The dorsal horn (sensory) is spared, explaining the lack of sensory deficits.

question:
a. Which of the labelled parts (A, B, C, D, E) in the provided brain cross-section transmits visual fibers?
b. Name the artery supplying the area marked as C (Be specific).
correctAnswer: a. E (Retrolenticular part of the internal capsule — carries optic radiation fibers from the lateral geniculate body to the primary visual cortex in the occipital lobe). b. Lenticulostriate branch of the middle cerebral artery (supplies the posterior limb of the internal capsule, area C).
sourceAnswer: a. E (Retrolenticular part of the internal capsule in the Occipital lobe). b. Lenticulostriate branch of middle cerebral artery (supplies posterior limb of the internal capsule).
explanation: The internal capsule is divided into: anterior limb, genu, posterior limb, retrolenticular part, and sublenticular part. Visual (optic radiation) fibers run in the retrolenticular and sublenticular parts. The posterior limb (carrying corticospinal fibers) is supplied by the lenticulostriate branches of the MCA.

question: The nerve conduction velocity of the median nerve is determined as shown in the diagram. The nerve is stimulated at S in the elbow and the response recorded at R in the wrist, 28 cm away. If the latency is 4 milliseconds, calculate the conduction velocity of the nerve in meters per second (m/sec).
explanation: Nerve conduction velocity = Distance / Time. Distance = 28 cm = 0.28 m. Time = 4 milliseconds = 0.004 seconds. NCV = 0.28 m ÷ 0.004 s = 70 m/sec. Normal median nerve NCV is approximately 50-70 m/sec. Formula: v = d/t.
